Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Paper: Noetel et al., 2024, BMJ
Summary: Network MA, 218 RCTs / 14,170 participants; walking-jogging reduced depression vs active controls (g −0.62), effect grows with intensity.
Claude Reasoning: Highest evidence form (network MA of RCTs) at the largest scale available, and the benefit holds against active controls — a clinically meaningful effect that clears the bar for the top tier.

Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Paper: Leung et al., 2025, Br. J. Sports Medicine
Summary: SR/MA, 36 RCTs / 2,500 patients with chronic illness + comorbid depression; aerobic exercise g −0.73, with dose-response.
Claude Reasoning: Independent replication in a harder-to-treat population with a moderate-large effect and a dose gradient — shows the benefit generalizes, which is what a Strong ranking requires.

Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Paper: Du et al., 2026, BMC Public Health
Summary: Bayesian dose-response MA, 12 RCTs / 723 adolescents; g −0.28, benefit peaking ~740 MET-min/week.
Claude Reasoning: A third independent MA in a different age group confirms a significant effect and dose-response — cross-population consistency reinforces the robustness expected at the top tier.

Description: Train major muscle groups 2–3 times per week with progressive overload and enough recovery.
Paper: Currier et al., 2023, British Journal of Sports Medicine
Summary: Bayesian network meta-analysis, 178 strength/119 hypertrophy RCTs (~5097); all resistance-training doses beat no-exercise (SMD up to 1.60 for strength).
Claude Reasoning: Largest, most rigorous network meta-analysis of resistance training in healthy adults shows every prescription robustly increases strength and hypertrophy versus controls, directly supporting a Strong ranking.

Description: Train major muscle groups 2–3 times per week with progressive overload and enough recovery.
Paper: Lopez et al., 2020, Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise
Summary: Network meta-analysis, 28 studies/747 adults; high/moderate loads beat low load for strength (SMD 0.34–0.63); hypertrophy load-independent.
Claude Reasoning: Adds confirmation across training loads that resistance training reliably builds strength and muscle, with stronger strength gains under progressive higher loads.

Description: Write the situation, emotion, automatic thought, evidence for and against it, a balanced thought, and the next action.
Paper: Cuijpers et al., 2023, World Psychiatry
Summary: Meta-analysis, 409 RCTs/52,702 patients; CBT moderate-to-large effect vs control (g=0.79), sustained at 6–12 mo follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: The largest-ever meta-analysis of CBT for depression shows robust efficacy of the cognitive-behavioral package the thought record operationalizes; construct is the broader CBT protocol, not the worksheet alone.

Description: Write the situation, emotion, automatic thought, evidence for and against it, a balanced thought, and the next action.
Paper: Ciharova et al., 2021, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Summary: Network meta-analysis, 45 RCTs/3382 patients; cognitive restructuring superior to care-as-usual (SMD 0.57) and waitlist (1.20) for depression.
Claude Reasoning: Isolates cognitive restructuring—the core of the thought record—and shows it is effective on its own, a closer construct match than the full CBT package.

Description: Record the Activating event, Belief, and Consequence, then dispute the belief and choose a more useful response.
Paper: David et al., 2017, Journal of Clinical Psychology
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis, 84 studies; REBT showed medium effects vs other interventions on outcomes (d=0.58) and on irrational beliefs (d=0.70).
Claude Reasoning: The ABC model is the defining technique of REBT; this is the most comprehensive meta-analysis of REBT and confirms it reduces irrational beliefs and improves outcomes.

Description: Record the Activating event, Belief, and Consequence, then dispute the belief and choose a more useful response.
Paper: Gonzalez et al., 2004, Journal of Emotional and Behavioral Disorders
Summary: Meta-analysis, 19 studies; REBT showed a positive, significant overall effect in children/adolescents, largest on disruptive behaviors.
Claude Reasoning: Extends REBT/ABC efficacy evidence to youth across multiple outcome domains, broadening support for the technique's Strong ranking.

Description: Identify a distorted thought, test it against evidence, and replace it with a more accurate belief.
Paper: Ciharova et al., 2021, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Summary: Network meta-analysis, 45 RCTs/3382 patients; cognitive restructuring beat care-as-usual (SMD 0.57) and waitlist (1.20), equal to BA and full CBT.
Claude Reasoning: Directly isolates cognitive restructuring as a standalone component and shows it is effective for adult depression, an exact construct match for a Strong ranking.

Description: Identify a distorted thought, test it against evidence, and replace it with a more accurate belief.
Paper: Cuijpers et al., 2023, World Psychiatry
Summary: Meta-analysis, 409 RCTs/52,702 patients; CBT (built on cognitive restructuring) had g=0.79 vs control, durable at follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: The broader CBT evidence base, of which restructuring is the cognitive core, reinforces that evidence-testing of thoughts is a strongly supported active ingredient.

Description: Schedule small rewarding or valued actions before motivation appears, then track mood afterward.
Paper: Stein et al., 2019, Psychological Medicine
Summary: Meta-analysis, 28 RCTs; behavioral activation beat inactive control for depression (g=0.83), anxiety (g=0.37) and activation (g=0.64).
Claude Reasoning: A direct, well-powered meta-analysis of behavioral activation showing large effects on depression confirms the technique works, supporting a Strong ranking.

Description: Schedule small rewarding or valued actions before motivation appears, then track mood afterward.
Paper: Cuijpers et al., 2023, Psychotherapy Research
Summary: Meta-analysis, 22 RCTs/819 patients; individual BA had a large effect vs controls (g=0.85; g=0.56 in low-risk-of-bias studies).
Claude Reasoning: Focuses on individual-format BA and confirms a robust effect even when limited to low-risk-of-bias trials, strengthening the evidence base.

Description: Build a fear hierarchy and repeatedly face the easiest feared situation until anxiety drops before advancing.
Paper: Odgers et al., 2022, Behaviour Research and Therapy
Summary: Meta-analysis, 85 comparisons/1758 patients; in vivo exposure for specific phobia gave large pre-post effects on approach and symptoms.
Claude Reasoning: Directly evaluates graded in vivo exposure for phobia and finds large effects regardless of session format, an on-target match for a Strong ranking.

Description: Build a fear hierarchy and repeatedly face the easiest feared situation until anxiety drops before advancing.
Paper: Carl et al., 2018, Journal of Anxiety Disorders
Summary: Meta-analysis, 30 RCTs/1057 patients; virtual-reality exposure had large effect vs waitlist (g=0.90) and matched in vivo exposure (g=-0.07).
Claude Reasoning: Shows exposure therapy's efficacy generalizes across anxiety disorders and delivery formats, reinforcing exposure as a strongly supported mechanism.

Description: Trigger the obsession or fear while deliberately not doing the compulsion, reassurance, avoidance, or escape behavior.
Paper: Reid et al., 2021, Comprehensive Psychiatry
Summary: Meta-analysis, 36 RCTs/2020 patients; CBT with ERP had large effect on OCD vs all controls (g=0.74) and vs placebo (g=1.13).
Claude Reasoning: Large, preregistered meta-analysis of ERP for OCD—an exact construct match—shows a large effect, though authors note efficacy depends on comparator and researcher allegiance.

Description: Trigger the obsession or fear while deliberately not doing the compulsion, reassurance, avoidance, or escape behavior.
Paper: Song et al., 2022, Psychiatry Research
Summary: Meta-analysis, 39 RCTs/1793 patients; ERP improved OCD overall (g=0.37), with large effects vs placebo (g=0.97) and vs drug (g=0.59).
Claude Reasoning: Independent meta-analysis confirms ERP's superiority over placebo and pharmacotherapy controls for OCD, corroborating the Strong ranking.

Description: Define the problem, generate options, choose one, act, and review the result.
Paper: Cuijpers et al., 2018, European Psychiatry
Summary: Meta-analysis, 30 RCTs/3530 patients; PST beat control (g=0.79 overall; g=0.34 in low-risk-of-bias studies), comparable to other therapies.
Claude Reasoning: Most up-to-date meta-analysis of PST for depression—an exact construct match—shows a significant effect even in low-bias trials, supporting a Strong ranking.

Description: Define the problem, generate options, choose one, act, and review the result.
Paper: Bell & D'Zurilla, 2009, Clinical Psychology Review
Summary: Meta-analysis, 21 samples; PST equaled other psychotherapies and medication and beat no-treatment/attention controls for depression.
Claude Reasoning: Earlier independent meta-analysis confirms PST efficacy and shows the full four-skill package drives larger effects, reinforcing the technique's components.

Description: Postpone worries to a scheduled 10–20 minute window and redirect attention when they arise outside it.
Paper: Dippel et al., 2023, International Journal of Cognitive Therapy
Summary: Meta-analysis, 7 RCTs/999 participants; worry postponement reduced daily worry duration (d=0.31) and frequency (d=0.19) vs worry monitoring.
Claude Reasoning: Only meta-analysis specific to worry postponement; effects are significant but small, so evidence for this standalone technique is best described as positive yet modest.

Description: Postpone worries to a scheduled 10–20 minute window and redirect attention when they arise outside it.
Paper: Krzikalla et al., 2024, Clinical Psychology in Europe
Summary: Waitlist-controlled RCT, n=82 (GAD/hypochondriasis); worry postponement significantly lowered worry in GAD (large pre-post effect), sustained at 4 weeks.
Claude Reasoning: A controlled RCT in a clinical GAD sample shows worry postponement reduces worry, adding rigorous single-trial support beyond the small meta-analytic effect.

Description: Tense and release each major muscle group in sequence while noticing the contrast.
Paper: Donato et al., 2026, Journal of Psychosomatic Research
Summary: Meta-analysis, 31 RCTs/2277 patients; PMR improved sleep quality (SMD -1.74) and reduced anxiety (SMD -1.11) vs control (high heterogeneity).
Claude Reasoning: Most recent and largest meta-analysis dedicated to progressive muscle relaxation, showing significant effects on anxiety and sleep, supporting a Strong ranking.

Description: Tense and release each major muscle group in sequence while noticing the contrast.
Paper: Tan et al., 2022, Complementary Therapies in Clinical Practice
Summary: Meta-analysis, 12 RCTs/1147 cancer patients; PMR training significantly reduced anxiety (SMD -1.32) and pain and improved quality of life.
Claude Reasoning: Confirms PMR's anxiety-reducing effect in a distinct clinical population, broadening the evidence base; effect is shown in cancer patients specifically.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Paper: Goyal et al., 2014, JAMA Internal Medicine
Summary: SR/MA of 47 active-control RCTs / 3,515 participants; moderate evidence mindfulness meditation improves anxiety (0.38), depression (0.30), pain.
Claude Reasoning: The most conservative possible test — only active-control RCTs, which strip out placebo/expectancy — still finds durable benefit. Surviving that bar is the strongest justification for a Strong ranking.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Paper: Galante et al., 2023, Nature Mental Health
Summary: Individual-participant-data MA, 13 RCTs / 2,371 participants; distress SMD −0.32 at 1–6 months; confidence rated "high."
Claude Reasoning: IPD meta-analysis is the gold-standard synthesis form, and the authors themselves grade confidence as high — top-tier methodological pedigree behind the ranking.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Paper: Galante et al., 2021, PLoS Medicine
Summary: SR/MA, 136 RCTs / 11,605 participants; vs no intervention depression SMD −0.53, anxiety −0.56.
Claude Reasoning: Establishes breadth (136 RCTs) of benefit; the fact that effects shrink vs active controls is exactly why the ranking is Strong rather than over-stated, so it supports the placement honestly.

Description: Limit time in bed to actual sleep time, then gradually expand the window as sleep efficiency improves.
Paper: Maurer et al., 2021, Sleep Medicine Reviews
Summary: Meta-analysis, 8 RCTs; standalone sleep restriction therapy gave large effects on insomnia severity (g=-0.93) and sleep efficiency (g=0.91).
Claude Reasoning: Dedicated meta-analysis of standalone sleep restriction therapy shows large effects comparable to full CBT-I, an exact construct match for a Strong ranking.

Description: Limit time in bed to actual sleep time, then gradually expand the window as sleep efficiency improves.
Paper: Wei et al., 2025, Journal of Sleep Research
Summary: Meta-analysis, 9 RCTs/1239 patients; single-component SRT significantly improved insomnia severity, sleep efficiency and onset latency vs control.
Claude Reasoning: Newer, larger meta-analysis of single-component SRT replicates the benefits and adds sustained long-term effects on several outcomes, reinforcing the ranking.

Description: Write a specific 'If situation X happens, then I will do behavior Y' plan.
Paper: Gollwitzer & Sheeran, 2006, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 94 independent tests; forming if-then implementation intentions had a medium-to-large effect (d=.65) on goal attainment.
Claude Reasoning: The foundational meta-analysis of the exact if-then construct, aggregating 94 tests with a robust medium-to-large effect, directly establishes that implementation intentions improve goal attainment.

Description: Write a specific 'If situation X happens, then I will do behavior Y' plan.
Paper: Wang et al., 2021, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 21 studies / 15,907 participants; mental contrasting with implementation intentions aided goal attainment (g=0.34), small-to-medium.
Claude Reasoning: A large, recent meta-analysis confirms the if-then planning component (combined with mental contrasting) produces reliable goal-attainment gains across nearly 16,000 participants.

Description: Write a specific 'If situation X happens, then I will do behavior Y' plan.
Paper: Breitwieser et al., 2026, British Journal of Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 42 studies / N=12,957 children; implementation intentions had a small-to-medium effect on goal achievement (g=0.31).
Claude Reasoning: A large registered-report meta-analysis replicates the effect in a distinct population (children), strengthening evidence that the if-then technique generalizes beyond adults.

Description: Set a hard but reachable target and compare progress against it regularly.
Paper: Epton et al., 2017, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of RCTs, 384 effect sizes / N=16,523; goal setting had a small positive effect (d=.34), strongest when goals were difficult.
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis isolating the unique effect of goal setting on behavior change, confirming difficult, specific goals (the core construct) work across many behaviors.

Description: Set a hard but reachable target and compare progress against it regularly.
Paper: Mento et al., 1987, Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
Summary: Meta-analysis (1966-1984); strong support for goal difficulty and specificity, and for specific-hard goals + feedback outperforming goals without feedback.
Claude Reasoning: Classic meta-analysis directly testing the goal-plus-feedback construct, showing specific hard goals combined with feedback beat the same goals without feedback.

Description: Set a hard but reachable target and compare progress against it regularly.
Paper: Kleingeld et al., 2011, Journal of Applied Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, k=49; specific difficult goals yielded much higher performance than nonspecific goals (d=0.80 for specific-difficult; d=0.56 overall).
Claude Reasoning: Large meta-analysis confirming the goal-specificity/difficulty effect with a large effect size, though in group-performance settings rather than personal health behavior.

Description: Record the target behavior or outcome daily so patterns become visible.
Paper: Berry et al., 2021, Obesity Reviews
Summary: Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs; digital self-monitoring of diet and activity supported weight loss (MD -2.87 kg) and increased activity (SMD 0.44).
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis isolating self-monitoring (recording the target behavior) and showing significant weight, activity, and intake effects directly matches the construct.

Description: Record the target behavior or outcome daily so patterns become visible.
Paper: Cavero-Redondo et al., 2020, Nutrients
Summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis, 20 studies; mHealth lifestyle self-monitoring produced moderate weight loss and higher adherence vs other methods.
Claude Reasoning: Independent meta-analysis of self-monitoring interventions showing a moderate weight reduction and superior adherence, reinforcing that recording the behavior drives change.

Description: Review tracked data, identify the next bottleneck, and change one variable at a time.
Paper: Krukowski et al., 2024, Int. Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis of RCTs, 19 studies / 3,261 participants; activity interventions with feedback outperformed those without (d=0.73).
Claude Reasoning: RCT meta-analysis that isolates reviewing tracked data and acting on it (feedback vs no feedback), directly supporting the feedback-loop construct with a large effect.

Description: Review tracked data, identify the next bottleneck, and change one variable at a time.
Paper: Wisniewski et al., 2020, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 435 studies / >61,000 learners; feedback had a medium effect (d=0.48) on learning, larger when it conveyed rich corrective information.
Claude Reasoning: Very large meta-analysis showing acting on informative feedback drives improvement; from the education domain rather than health-habit self-tracking, so construct match is broader.

Description: Stake money, reputation, or access so quitting has a real cost.
Paper: Coupe et al., 2019, BMC Public Health
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis; 3 RCTs (N=409) found commitment devices increased short-term weight loss by 1.5 kg, sustained ~1.7 kg at 12 months.
Claude Reasoning: Meta-analysis of RCTs isolating commitment devices (behavioral contracts/public stakes) shows significant weight-loss benefit, most effective when made publicly, matching the construct.

Description: Stake money, reputation, or access so quitting has a real cost.
Paper: Rogers et al., 2014, JAMA
Summary: JAMA review of commitment/deposit contracts; voluntary stakes with penalties help people lose weight, improve diet, exercise more, and quit smoking.
Claude Reasoning: Authoritative JAMA synthesis defining and reviewing commitment devices (staking money with consequences for failure), the exact mechanism described by the technique.

Description: Repeat one small behavior after the same cue in the same context until it becomes automatic.
Paper: Singh et al., 2024, Healthcare
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis, 20 studies / 2,601 participants; context-repeated behaviors significantly increased automaticity (SMD 0.69).
Claude Reasoning: Meta-analysis directly measuring habit automaticity gains from cue-consistent repetition, the precise construct of stable-cue habit formation.

Description: Repeat one small behavior after the same cue in the same context until it becomes automatic.
Paper: Lally & Gardner, 2013, Health Psychology Review
Summary: Review of habit-formation evidence and techniques; context-dependent repetition builds automaticity, making behaviors resistant to motivational lapses.
Claude Reasoning: Foundational evidence-based review establishing that repeating a behavior after a consistent cue builds automaticity, the mechanism underpinning stable-cue habits.

Description: Attach a new behavior immediately after an existing reliable routine.
Paper: Keller et al., 2021, British Journal of Health Psychology
Summary: RCT, N=192 over 84 days; anchoring a new behavior to an existing routine cue built automaticity as well as time-based cues (median 59 days to peak).
Claude Reasoning: RCT directly testing routine-based (existing-routine) cue planning, the precise habit-stacking construct, showing it reliably builds automaticity.

Description: Attach a new behavior immediately after an existing reliable routine.
Paper: Linnemayr et al., 2024, Journal of the International AIDS Society
Summary: RCT, N=155; anchoring pill-taking to an existing daily routine (plus incentives) raised adherence in line with the anchor by ~12-14 percentage points.
Claude Reasoning: RCT applying the anchoring/habit-stacking strategy (pairing a new behavior with an existing routine) to medication adherence, showing durable gains that match the construct.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Paper: Jachimowicz et al., 2019, Behavioural Public Policy
Summary: MA of 58 default studies / n=73,675 (on-target to defaults); d=0.68, with honest variability (a few null, two negative).
Claude Reasoning: Construct-matched MA with a large average effect but real heterogeneity (some null/negative). That combination — strong but not universal — is precisely what a Moderate-to-Strong (not Strong) ranking should reflect.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Paper: Mertens et al., 2021, PNAS
Summary: MA, 200+ studies / n≈2.1M; choice architecture d=0.43; decision-structure nudges (incl. defaults) strongest category.
Claude Reasoning: Huge cross-domain MA confirms the broader family works and that defaults are its strongest sub-class — situating the technique among the better-evidenced behavioral tools, consistent with a high-but-not-top ranking.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Paper: Davidai et al., 2012, PNAS
Summary: Classic opt-in vs opt-out organ-donation studies showing how large a participation shift a default produces.
Claude Reasoning: Demonstrates mechanism and magnitude in a high-stakes real-world setting, evidencing external validity — corroborates that defaults reliably move behavior.

Description: Join or build a group where the desired behavior is normal, visible, and reinforced.
Paper: Jensen et al., 2024, International Journal of Obesity
Summary: Meta-analysis of RCTs, 24 trials / 4,919 adults; social-support-based interventions improved weight loss at end of intervention and 3- and 6-month follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis isolating a social-connectedness component (peers/groups) and finding significant weight-loss benefits, directly matching the group-support construct.

Description: Join or build a group where the desired behavior is normal, visible, and reinforced.
Paper: Yuan et al., 2023, Nicotine & Tobacco Research
Summary: Meta-analysis, 15 RCTs / 8,573 participants; peer-support interventions increased smoking abstinence (pooled RR 1.34).
Claude Reasoning: Large RCT meta-analysis showing peer/group support raises quit rates, evidencing that a supportive group where the behavior is reinforced changes behavior.

Description: Join or build a group where the desired behavior is normal, visible, and reinforced.
Paper: Gómez-Pardo et al., 2016, Journal of the American College of Cardiology
Summary: RCT, N=543; a peer-group intervention improved a composite cardiovascular health score vs self-management (difference 0.75), notably tobacco cessation.
Claude Reasoning: Rigorous single RCT showing a peer-group format where healthy behaviors are normalized and reinforced improved multiple risk factors, matching the group-support construct.

Description: Slow down, attend to pleasant details, and deliberately extend the positive experience.
Paper: Chen et al., 2026, Applied Psychology: Health and Well-Being
Summary: Meta-analysis, 20 RCTs / 4,805 participants; savoring interventions had a moderate effect on emotional outcomes (g=0.51), robust to publication-bias checks.
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis of the exact savoring construct (amplifying/extending positive experience) with a moderate, bias-robust effect across nearly 5,000 participants.

Description: Slow down, attend to pleasant details, and deliberately extend the positive experience.
Paper: Kumar et al., 2024, Behaviour Research and Therapy
Summary: RCT, N=60 students; behavioral activation plus savoring raised daily positive affect via experience sampling vs an emotional-awareness control.
Claude Reasoning: Single RCT directly testing a savoring component on positive affect, complementing the meta-analysis with experience-sampling evidence that savoring extends positive experience.

Description: Spend regular time walking, sitting, or exercising in green or blue spaces.
Paper: Coventry et al., 2021, SSM - Population Health
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis, 50 studies (16 RCTs); outdoor nature-based activity improved depressive mood, anxiety, and positive affect (SMDs ~0.5-0.95).
Claude Reasoning: Meta-analysis of RCTs on structured green/blue-space activity (walking, gardening, green exercise) directly matches the construct and shows consistent mental-health benefits.

Description: Spend regular time walking, sitting, or exercising in green or blue spaces.
Paper: White et al., 2021, Scientific Reports
Summary: 18-country survey, N=16,307; more frequent recreational visits to green and blue spaces were linked to higher well-being and lower mental distress.
Claude Reasoning: Large multi-country observational study confirming that regular green/blue-space visits track with better well-being, adding cross-cultural breadth though it is correlational.

Description: Pick a realistic daily step target, track it, and increase it gradually when it becomes easy.
Paper: Chaudhry et al., 2020, Int. J. Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Summary: Meta-analysis of 70 RCTs (57 in meta-analysis, 16,355 adults): step-count monitoring raised activity by ~1,126 steps/day at 4 months, persisting to 3-4 years.
Claude Reasoning: A large, low-risk-of-bias meta-analysis of RCTs showing consistent short- and long-term step increases directly supports a Moderate-to-Strong rating for tracking a step target.

Description: Pick a realistic daily step target, track it, and increase it gradually when it becomes easy.
Paper: Wang et al., 2025, Digital Health
Summary: Meta-analysis of 34 studies in children/adolescents: step-count monitoring increased daily steps by ~1,589 steps/day; pedometers outperformed other devices.
Claude Reasoning: Extends the evidence to younger populations with a PRISMA meta-analysis showing a sizeable step increase, reinforcing robustness of the technique across ages.

Description: Breathe low into the belly at roughly 5–6 breaths per minute for several minutes.
Paper: Laborde et al., 2022, Psychophysiology
Summary: RCT (N=112): slow-paced breathing at 6 cycles/min, with or without biofeedback, lowered emotional arousal and perceived stress and raised vagal HRV (RMSSD).
Claude Reasoning: A controlled experiment isolating slow-paced breathing shows direct physiological and stress-reduction effects, matching the technique's ~6 breaths/min target.

Description: Breathe low into the belly at roughly 5–6 breaths per minute for several minutes.
Paper: Sévoz-Couche & Laborde, 2022, Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews
Summary: Review of slow-paced breathing showing resonance-frequency breathing (~0.1 Hz) improves vagal HRV, baroreflex sensitivity, and stress management.
Claude Reasoning: Synthesizes mechanism and clinical findings explaining why ~6 breaths/min works, supporting the plausibility and consistency behind a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Specify exactly when, where, how, and with what materials you will act.
Paper: Gollwitzer & Sheeran, 2006, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 94 independent tests: forming if-then implementation intentions had a medium-to-large effect (d=.65) on goal attainment.
Claude Reasoning: The foundational meta-analysis of the exact construct (specifying when/where/how) shows a sizeable effect on behavior, strongly supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Specify exactly when, where, how, and with what materials you will act.
Paper: Sheeran et al., 2024, European Review of Social Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 642 tests: implementation intentions improved cognitive, affective, and behavioural outcomes (.27<=d<=.66), largest with if-then format and rehearsal.
Claude Reasoning: A large, recent meta-analysis confirms the effect and identifies that the contingent (if-then) format used in action planning maximizes efficacy.

Description: Specify exactly when, where, how, and with what materials you will act.
Paper: da Silva et al., 2018, PLoS ONE
Summary: Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs: implementation-intention reinforcement increased physical activity (SMD=0.25, 95% CI 0.05–0.45) versus controls.
Claude Reasoning: An RCT-only meta-analysis demonstrates that action planning produces real behavior change in an applied health domain, reinforcing the rating.

Description: Pair a beneficial task with an indulgence you only allow during that task.
Paper: Milkman et al., 2014, Management Science
Summary: RCT field experiment: gym-only access to tempting audiobooks raised gym visits 51% (full) and 29% (intermediate) vs control; 61% later paid for the commitment device.
Claude Reasoning: The seminal randomized field experiment defining and testing temptation bundling shows large initial behavior gains, directly supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Pair a beneficial task with an indulgence you only allow during that task.
Paper: Kirgios et al., 2020, Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
Summary: RCT (N=6,792): teaching temptation bundling plus audiobooks boosted weekly workouts 10–12% during and up to 17 weeks post-intervention.
Claude Reasoning: A large, well-powered RCT replicates and extends the effect over a sustained follow-up, strengthening confidence in the technique.

Description: Shrink the behavior until it feels easy, perform it after a cue, and briefly celebrate completion.
Paper: Keller et al., 2021, British Journal of Health Psychology
Summary: RCT (N=192): linking a small everyday behavior to a routine or time cue and repeating it built automaticity; repeated cue-based enactment was the key predictor.
Claude Reasoning: An RCT directly testing cue-anchored repetition of a small behavior (the core of Tiny Habits) shows habit/automaticity gains, supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Shrink the behavior until it feels easy, perform it after a cue, and briefly celebrate completion.
Paper: Ma et al., 2023, Int. J. Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Summary: Meta-analysis of 10 studies: habit-formation interventions significantly increased habit strength (SMD=0.31, 95% CI 0.14–0.48).
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of the underlying habit-formation mechanism confirms that cue-based repetition reliably builds automaticity, supporting the rating.

Description: Put every open loop into one trusted inbox, clarify the next action, and review it regularly.
Paper: Uhlig et al., 2023, Journal of Occupational and Organizational Psychology
Summary: Field experiment (208 people, 947 weekly entries): manipulating weekly planning reduced unfinished tasks and rumination and increased cognitive flexibility.
Claude Reasoning: A randomized field experiment shows that capturing and planning open tasks reduces mental preoccupation and unfinished-task load, the core claimed benefit of GTD capture.

Description: Put every open loop into one trusted inbox, clarify the next action, and review it regularly.
Paper: Masicampo & Baumeister, 2011, Journal of Experimental Social Psychology
Summary: Five experiments: unfulfilled goals impaired executive-function tasks (anagrams, impulse control); effects vanished once the goal was fulfilled.
Claude Reasoning: Establishes the mechanism (open loops consume cognitive resources via the Zeigarnik effect) that GTD capture targets, supporting why externalizing open loops helps.

Description: Change your surroundings so the desired behavior is easy and the undesired behavior is inconvenient.
Paper: Mertens et al., 2022, PNAS
Summary: Meta-analysis of 200+ studies (450+ effect sizes, N~2.1M): choice-architecture/nudge interventions changed behavior with d=0.43–0.45; decision-structure nudges strongest.
Claude Reasoning: A very large meta-analysis of choice-architecture interventions (restructuring the environment to ease desired choices) shows a reliable moderate effect, supporting the rating.

Description: Change your surroundings so the desired behavior is easy and the undesired behavior is inconvenient.
Paper: Broers et al., 2017, European Journal of Public Health
Summary: Meta-analysis (14 studies): nudging choice architecture increased fruit/vegetable choice (d=0.30), with placement changes most effective (d=0.39).
Claude Reasoning: Shows that altering the physical environment (e.g., placement/positioning) reliably shifts behavior, a concrete instance of environmental design supporting the rating.

Description: Write the life domains that matter most and choose actions aligned with them.
Paper: Epton et al., 2015, Health Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 144 experiments: reflecting on important values (self-affirmation) had small but reliable effects on message acceptance, intentions, and behavior (d=.32).
Claude Reasoning: A large meta-analysis of the values-reflection construct shows consistent positive effects on behavior, supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating; effects are small in magnitude.

Description: Write the life domains that matter most and choose actions aligned with them.
Paper: Yan et al., 2023, Applied Psychology: Health and Well-Being
Summary: Large RCT (N=2,234 adolescents): a values-affirmation writing intervention improved life satisfaction, mental health, and self-esteem versus control.
Claude Reasoning: A large RCT of writing about core values shows direct well-being benefits, matching the technique and reinforcing the rating despite null effects on clinical measures.

Description: Label thoughts as thoughts, such as 'I'm having the thought that…,' instead of treating them as commands.
Paper: Larsson et al., 2016, Behavior Modification
Summary: 5-day RCT: a cognitive defusion strategy reduced believability and discomfort of negative thoughts and cut their frequency more than restructuring or control.
Claude Reasoning: A randomized comparison directly testing the defusion technique (relating to thoughts as thoughts) shows superiority over control, supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Label thoughts as thoughts, such as 'I'm having the thought that…,' instead of treating them as commands.
Paper: Masuda et al., 2004, Behaviour Research and Therapy
Summary: Eight single-case alternating-treatment designs: a word-repetition defusion technique reduced discomfort and believability of negative thoughts more than distraction or thought control.
Claude Reasoning: A controlled, oft-cited dismantling study isolates defusion and shows it specifically lowers thought believability, supporting the technique's efficacy.

Description: Label thoughts as thoughts, such as 'I'm having the thought that…,' instead of treating them as commands.
Paper: Ritzert et al., 2015, Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science
Summary: RCT (N=65 spider-fearful): a defusion intervention produced greater reductions in thought believability and more flexible responding than distraction or control.
Claude Reasoning: A randomized experiment shows defusion reduces thought believability and increases psychological flexibility, the proposed mechanism behind the rating.

Description: Write three specific things you feel grateful for each day.
Paper: Cregg & Cheavens, 2021, Journal of Happiness Studies
Summary: Meta-analysis (k=27, N=3,675): gratitude interventions had small effects on depression (g=-0.29) and anxiety symptoms at post-test, sustained at follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of the exact construct (writing gratitude lists) shows reliable, if modest, symptom reductions, honestly supporting a Moderate-to-Strong rating.

Description: Write three specific things you feel grateful for each day.
Paper: Cunha et al., 2019, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: RCT (N=1,337): writing daily gratitude lists for 14 days increased positive affect, happiness, and life satisfaction and reduced negative affect and depression.
Claude Reasoning: A large RCT of daily gratitude-list writing (the exact technique) shows broad well-being gains, strengthening the evidence for the rating.

Description: Write three specific things you feel grateful for each day.
Paper: Lai & O'Carroll, 2017, Health Psychology Update
Summary: RCT (N=108): a 21-day daily gratitude journal ('three good things') increased state gratitude and positive affect versus a no-activity control.
Claude Reasoning: A randomized trial of the specific 'three good things' journaling format shows affect and gratitude gains, closely matching the technique and supporting the rating.

Description: Each evening, list three good events from the day and your role in them (or why they happened).
Paper: Seligman et al., 2005, American Psychologist
Summary: 6-group placebo-controlled internet RCT; the Three Good Things exercise lastingly increased happiness and decreased depressive symptoms for 6 months.
Claude Reasoning: This is the foundational randomized, placebo-controlled trial that defined and validated the exact 'three good things' exercise, directly supporting a Moderate-to-Strong ranking.

Description: Each evening, list three good events from the day and your role in them (or why they happened).
Paper: Gold et al., 2023, Annals of Family Medicine
Summary: RCT, N=223 health care workers; a texted 'Three Good Things' practice produced small but significant gains in positive affect at 3 months.
Claude Reasoning: A modern, well-retained RCT replicating the specific 3GT protocol in a real-world sample, reinforcing the technique's efficacy with realistic (modest) effects.

Description: Each evening, list three good events from the day and your role in them (or why they happened).
Paper: Lai & O'Carroll, 2017, Health Psychology Update
Summary: RCT, N=108; a 21-day 'three good things' gratitude journal raised state gratitude and positive affect vs. control, which declined.
Claude Reasoning: A dedicated RCT of the exact daily journaling format showing affect benefits, adding independent confirmation across a non-clinical sample.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Lindsay et al., 2021, Physical Education & Sport Pedagogy
Summary: SR/MA, 36 studies / 1,449 participants; mental imagery improves performance g=0.476 (imagery+physical 0.579; imagery alone 0.298).
Claude Reasoning: Construct-matched to process rehearsal (motor imagery, not outcome fantasy) with a moderate effect on real performance — direct, on-target support justifying a strong-but-not-top tier.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Toth et al., 2020, Psychology of Sport & Exercise
Summary: Methodological replication of Driskell (1994); 37 studies; small but robust positive effect (r=0.131) after publication-bias correction.
Claude Reasoning: A deliberately skeptical, bias-corrected replication still finds a reliable effect; the modest residual size is exactly why Moderate-to-Strong rather than Strong is the honest call.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Simonsmeier et al., 2020, Int. Review of Sport & Exercise Psychology
Summary: MA of imagery interventions; medium effect d=0.431; imagery + physical practice beats physical alone.
Claude Reasoning: Independent MA reaches a similar medium effect and shows imagery adds value over physical practice alone — convergent evidence across reviews supports the ranking.

Description: State the facts, your feeling, your request, and your boundary in calm direct language.
Paper: Hagberg et al., 2023, Internet Interventions
Summary: RCT, N=210; an 8-week assertiveness training produced large gains in adaptive assertiveness (ES 0.95–1.73) sustained at 1-year follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: A preregistered RCT targeting the assertiveness construct directly, with large, durable effect sizes and reduced social anxiety, strongly supporting the ranking.

Description: State the facts, your feeling, your request, and your boundary in calm direct language.
Paper: Chen et al., 2023, Nurse Education Today
Summary: Systematic review & meta-analysis, 9 studies/N=804; assertive communication training improved speaking-up behaviors (SMD=0.58, 95% CI 0.14–1.03).
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis showing structured assertiveness scripting measurably changes real assertive behavior, adding higher-tier evidence in an applied domain.

Description: Reflect the other person's point and emotion before offering advice or disagreement.
Paper: Weger et al., 2014, International Journal of Listening
Summary: Experiment, N=115; partners receiving active-listening responses (paraphrase/reflect) felt significantly more understood than those given advice or acknowledgement.
Claude Reasoning: A controlled experiment isolating the exact reflect-before-responding behavior and showing it causally increases feeling understood, directly on-construct.

Description: Reflect the other person's point and emotion before offering advice or disagreement.
Paper: Bodie et al., 2015, Western Journal of Communication
Summary: RCT-style experiment, N=171; trained active listeners (open questions, paraphrase, reflect feelings) drove greater discloser emotional improvement.
Claude Reasoning: Randomized assignment to trained vs. untrained listeners shows the specific reflecting techniques improve emotional outcomes, supporting causal efficacy.

Description: Reflect the other person's point and emotion before offering advice or disagreement.
Paper: Kluger et al., 2023, Journal of Business and Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 664 effect sizes/400,020 obs; perceived listening correlated strongly with relationship quality (r=.51) and work outcomes (r=.39).
Claude Reasoning: A large registered meta-analysis establishing the broad association between high-quality listening and positive outcomes, though correlational rather than experimental.

Description: Get outdoor light soon after waking, ideally while moving and without sunglasses.
Paper: Lieverse et al., 2011, Archives of General Psychiatry
Summary: Double-blind RCT, N=89 elderly; 3 weeks of early-morning bright light improved depression scores 21% vs. placebo and advanced sleep timing.
Claude Reasoning: A rigorous placebo-controlled RCT of early-morning light showing mood and circadian phase benefits, the strongest design supporting morning light exposure.

Description: Get outdoor light soon after waking, ideally while moving and without sunglasses.
Paper: Li et al., 2026, Journal of Affective Disorders
Summary: Single-blind RCT, N=81; morning bright light (10,000 lx) reduced anhedonia and delayed melatonin peak vs. placebo in depressed patients.
Claude Reasoning: A recent timing-specific RCT showing morning (vs. evening) light uniquely benefits anhedonia via circadian phase shift, reinforcing the morning-timing emphasis.

Description: At day's end, note what happened, what worked, what failed, and tomorrow's priority.
Paper: Keiser & Arthur, 2021, Journal of Applied Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 61 studies (915 teams, 3,499 individuals); structured after-action review/debrief improved performance criteria (d=0.79).
Claude Reasoning: The largest meta-analysis of structured review-what-worked-and-failed reflection shows a large performance gain, closely matching the daily review construct.

Description: At day's end, note what happened, what worked, what failed, and tomorrow's priority.
Paper: Tannenbaum & Cerasoli, 2013, Human Factors
Summary: Meta-analysis, 46 samples (N=2,136); debriefs systematizing reflection and goal-setting improved performance ~25% (d=0.67) vs. controls.
Claude Reasoning: An independent meta-analysis confirming that structured reflect-and-set-next-priority reviews reliably boost performance for individuals and teams.

Description: At day's end, note what happened, what worked, what failed, and tomorrow's priority.
Paper: Maqsood et al., 2025, BMC Medical Education
Summary: RCT, N=68; daily written reflection improved post-test scores, with larger gains when paired with feedback (51% vs 69%).
Claude Reasoning: An RCT showing daily structured written reflection drives learning gains, providing individual-level experimental support for the practice.

Description: Draft a short statement of principles, roles, and long-term aims and revisit it regularly.
Paper: Manco & Hamby, 2021, American Journal of Health Promotion
Summary: Meta-analysis of 33 RCTs; meaning/purpose-in-life interventions raised meaning (SMD=0.85 vs passive, 0.32 vs active controls).
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of RCTs on articulating purpose and life aims—the core of a personal mission—shows robust gains, supporting a Moderate-to-Strong ranking.

Description: Draft a short statement of principles, roles, and long-term aims and revisit it regularly.
Paper: van Agteren et al., 2021, Journal of Happiness Studies
Summary: RCT; a 7-day meaning-and-purpose reflection program raised presence of meaning, life satisfaction, and positive affect vs. waitlist.
Claude Reasoning: A brief RCT directly prompting reflection on personal sources of meaning/purpose, mirroring the revisit-your-mission practice with measurable well-being gains.

Description: Rate major life domains, choose the lowest rated high-value domain, and set one action.
Paper: Russo-Netzer & Atad, 2024, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: RCT, N=476; choosing a valued life area, affirming it, and planning one action raised self-insight and coherence and cut psychopathology vs. controls.
Claude Reasoning: This RCT operationalizes the life-audit sequence almost exactly (rate/choose a valued domain, set one action) and shows well-being gains over affirmation-only and no-treatment controls.

Description: Rate major life domains, choose the lowest rated high-value domain, and set one action.
Paper: Karanasiou et al., 2025, Neuropsychological Rehabilitation
Summary: Pilot RCT, N=24 acquired brain injury; values-based goal-setting yielded greater wellbeing (d=0.30) and motivation than standard goal-setting.
Claude Reasoning: A randomized comparison showing embedding a chosen action in valued domains outperforms generic goal-setting on wellbeing, though small and in a clinical sample.

Description: Schedule distraction-free blocks for cognitively demanding work and remove communication channels.
Paper: Addas & Pinsonneault, 2018, MIS Quarterly
Summary: Two field studies (N=365 survey; N=212 diary) of sales pros; irrelevant e-mail interruptions raised workload and indirectly hurt performance.
Claude Reasoning: Large field evidence that removing irrelevant communication interruptions protects performance, directly supporting blocking communication channels for demanding work.

Description: Schedule distraction-free blocks for cognitively demanding work and remove communication channels.
Paper: May & Elder, 2018, Int. J. Educational Technology in Higher Education
Summary: Literature review; media multitasking and divided attention consistently impaired GPA, test performance, recall, and comprehension.
Claude Reasoning: Synthesized evidence that splitting attention across channels degrades cognitively demanding performance, justifying distraction-free blocks; review-level rather than single RCT.

Description: Schedule distraction-free blocks for cognitively demanding work and remove communication channels.
Paper: Schweisfurth & Greven, 2023, Organization Science
Summary: Natural experiment; intrusions (interruptions without idle time) reduced creative performance as employees switched attention away from work.
Claude Reasoning: Quasi-experimental evidence that unplanned intrusions impair cognitively demanding (creative) output via attention residue, supporting protecting focused blocks.

Description: Use bed only for sleep and sex, leave if awake too long, and keep a consistent wake time.
Paper: Jansson-Fröjmark et al., 2024, Journal of Sleep Research
Summary: Systematic review & meta-analysis, 11 RCTs; stimulus control improved sleep onset latency and total sleep time vs. passive controls (g=0.38–0.85).
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of RCTs of the exact stimulus-control protocol shows small-to-large sleep improvements, the strongest on-target evidence for this technique.

Description: Use bed only for sleep and sex, leave if awake too long, and keep a consistent wake time.
Paper: Verreault et al., 2024, Journal of Sleep Research
Summary: Systematic review & network meta-analysis, 23 randomized studies; stimulus control alone effectively improved insomnia vs. control conditions.
Claude Reasoning: A network meta-analysis isolating stimulus control as a standalone component confirms efficacy, strengthening the evidence base beyond bundled CBT-I.

Description: Use bed only for sleep and sex, leave if awake too long, and keep a consistent wake time.
Paper: Edinger et al., 2001, JAMA
Summary: Double-blind RCT, N=75; CBT incorporating stimulus control cut wake-after-sleep-onset 54% vs. 16% (relaxation) and 12% (placebo), durable at 6 months.
Claude Reasoning: A landmark placebo-controlled RCT where stimulus control is a core component, demonstrating large, durable sleep improvements.

Description: Wake at the same time every day, including weekends, to stabilize the sleep-wake rhythm.
Paper: Windred et al., 2024, Sleep
Summary: Prospective cohort, N=60,977 (UK Biobank accelerometry); higher sleep regularity predicted 20–48% lower all-cause mortality, beating sleep duration.
Claude Reasoning: A very large objective cohort showing consistent sleep-wake timing strongly predicts survival, the best-powered evidence that regularizing wake time matters.

Description: Wake at the same time every day, including weekends, to stabilize the sleep-wake rhythm.
Paper: Sletten et al., 2023, Sleep Health
Summary: National Sleep Foundation consensus statement (63 studies); expert panel concluded consistent sleep onset/offset timing is important for health and performance.
Claude Reasoning: A formal Delphi consensus of sleep experts endorses regular wake/sleep timing for health, providing authoritative synthesis backing the fixed-wake-time practice.

Description: Wake at the same time every day, including weekends, to stabilize the sleep-wake rhythm.
Paper: Chaput et al., 2020, Applied Physiology, Nutrition, and Metabolism
Summary: Systematic review, 41 studies/N=92,340; greater sleep-timing variability and irregular wake times were associated with adverse health outcomes.
Claude Reasoning: A large systematic review concluding consistent bedtimes/wake-up times favor health, supporting the rationale for a fixed wake time; observational evidence.

Description: Convert a vague aim into a Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound target.
Paper: Epton et al., 2017, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 141 papers, 384 effect sizes (N=16,523): goal setting had a small unique effect on behavior change (d=.34), strongest when goals were difficult and specific.
Claude Reasoning: Largest RCT-based meta-analysis of goal setting confirms specific, difficult goals (the S/M/A/T core of SMART) work, but the modest d=.34 effect fits a Moderate ranking.

Description: Convert a vague aim into a Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound target.
Paper: Mento et al., 1987, Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes
Summary: Classic meta-analysis (1966-1984) showing strong support for goal difficulty and goal specificity/difficulty effects on task performance, with feedback amplifying gains.
Claude Reasoning: Foundational meta-analysis establishing that specific, hard goals (SMART's S/M/A) outperform vague 'do-best' goals, supporting the construct while predating modern field RCTs.

Description: Convert a vague aim into a Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound target.
Paper: Williamson et al., 2022, International Review of Sport and Exercise Psychology
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis, 27 studies: goal setting improved performance, but specific goals (d=.37) did not outperform non-specific goals (d=.72).
Claude Reasoning: Adds honesty about limits: in sport contexts the 'Specific' element of SMART did not clearly beat non-specific goals, reflecting the mixed evidence behind a Moderate ranking.

Description: Name the Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, and Plan an if-then response to the obstacle.
Paper: Wang et al., 2021, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis, 21 studies / 24 effect sizes (N=15,907): MCII (the WOOP procedure) improved goal attainment with a small-to-medium effect (g=0.336).
Claude Reasoning: Directly meta-analyzes the exact Wish-Outcome-Obstacle-Plan (MCII) construct; the modest g=0.34 with noted publication bias matches a Moderate ranking.

Description: Name the Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, and Plan an if-then response to the obstacle.
Paper: Cross & Sheffield, 2019, Health Psychology Review
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis (N=1,528): mental contrasting improved health behavior (g=0.28 at 4 wks, 0.38 at 3 mo); adding implementation intentions did not clearly strengthen effects.
Claude Reasoning: Confirms the mental-contrasting-plus-if-then-plan construct works in the short term but with small effects, and questions the added value of the 'Plan' step, fitting Moderate.

Description: Name the Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, and Plan an if-then response to the obstacle.
Paper: Monin et al., 2021, Journals of Gerontology Series B
Summary: RCT, 45 dementia caregivers: WOOP training cut perceived stress (large effect) and raised quality of life and positive affect over 3 months versus education control.
Claude Reasoning: A single on-target RCT of the literal WOOP protocol showing benefits, but small sample and a specialized population keep the overall evidence at Moderate.

Description: Work on one task for about 25 minutes, take a short break, and repeat.
Paper: Ogut, 2025, BMC Medical Education
Summary: Scoping review, 32 studies (N=5,270); across 3 RCTs structured Pomodoro intervals cut fatigue ~20% and improved distractibility and motivation vs self-paced breaks.
Claude Reasoning: Broadest synthesis of the timed-work/short-break method; positive but built largely on observational studies and only 3 small RCTs, consistent with Moderate.

Description: Work on one task for about 25 minutes, take a short break, and repeat.
Paper: Biwer et al., 2023, British Journal of Educational Psychology
Summary: Experiment, 87 students: systematic 24-min-work/6-min-break ('Pomodoro') schedules yielded better mood and similar task completion in less time vs self-regulated breaks.
Claude Reasoning: Real-world experiment directly testing the Pomodoro schedule shows mood and efficiency benefits, but no difference in mental effort or completion, fitting a Moderate rating.

Description: Work on one task for about 25 minutes, take a short break, and repeat.
Paper: Smits et al., 2025, Behavioral Sciences
Summary: Experiment, 94 students: 25/5 Pomodoro breaks led to faster fatigue increase and motivation decline, with no overall differences in productivity, completion, or flow.
Claude Reasoning: A null/mixed-result experiment included for honesty: it tempers the technique's benefits and illustrates why evidence is Moderate rather than strong.

Description: Reserve calendar blocks in advance for specific work you want to do during them, for time to rest, for time to exercise, etc.
Paper: Häfner & Stock, 2010, The Journal of Psychology
Summary: RCT, 71 employees: time-management training (including scheduling techniques) raised perceived control of time and lowered stress, but did not improve performance indicators.
Claude Reasoning: On-target RCT of scheduling/time-management techniques showing benefits to control and stress but no performance gain, a mixed result consistent with Moderate.

Description: Reserve calendar blocks in advance for specific work you want to do during them, for time to rest, for time to exercise, etc.
Paper: Vizeshfar et al., 2022, Acute and Critical Care
Summary: RCT, 215 nurses: a time-management workshop significantly improved time-management and prioritization scores vs control, sustained at 3-month follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: Larger RCT confirming planning/scheduling training improves time-management skills, though it measures self-reported skill rather than time blocking specifically, supporting Moderate.

Description: Reserve calendar blocks in advance for specific work you want to do during them, for time to rest, for time to exercise, etc.
Paper: Lidström Holmqvist et al., 2026, Disability and Rehabilitation
Summary: Pragmatic RCT, 75 adults: a structured time-management program improved time-management, planning, and self-efficacy, but did not beat usual occupational therapy.
Claude Reasoning: On-construct RCT showing within-group gains but no advantage over an active control, illustrating the modest, comparator-dependent evidence behind a Moderate ranking.

Description: Tell someone the exact commitment and report completion on a fixed schedule.
Paper: Epton et al., 2017, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis (N=16,523): goal setting worked best when goals were set publicly and paired with external monitoring of behavior by others (overall d=.34).
Claude Reasoning: Directly tests accountability mechanisms (public goals, external monitoring by others) within a large meta-analysis; moderator effects are real but modest, fitting Moderate.

Description: Tell someone the exact commitment and report completion on a fixed schedule.
Paper: Munson et al., 2015, Proceedings of ACM CHI 2015
Summary: Randomized field experiment: publicly announcing weekly activity commitments elicited social support but made people less likely to commit, partly offsetting accountability benefits.
Claude Reasoning: On-target RCT of telling others your commitment and reporting results; the mixed finding (support vs reduced commitment) is exactly why accountability sits at Moderate.

Description: Tell someone the exact commitment and report completion on a fixed schedule.
Paper: Lee et al., 2021, Proceedings of ACM CHI 2021
Summary: Large stickK analysis: goal achievement depended on commitment-device configuration, with social (referee/supporter) plus financial stakes ('mixed') most beneficial.
Claude Reasoning: Real-world observational study of social commitment devices supports reporting to others, but its non-experimental design keeps the evidence Moderate.

Description: Notice suffering, remind yourself it is human, and speak to yourself as a good coach would.
Paper: Ferrari et al., 2019, Mindfulness
Summary: Meta-analysis of 27 RCTs: self-compassion interventions improved 11 outcomes, with moderate effects on self-compassion (g=0.75), stress (0.67), depression (0.66) and anxiety (0.57).
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis directly on self-compassion practices (which include the self-compassion break) shows consistent moderate effects, with noted publication bias supporting a Moderate ranking.

Description: Notice suffering, remind yourself it is human, and speak to yourself as a good coach would.
Paper: Han & Kim, 2023, Mindfulness
Summary: Meta-analysis of 56 RCTs: self-compassion interventions had small-to-medium effects on depression, anxiety and stress post-test, though overall risk of bias was high.
Claude Reasoning: Larger, more recent RCT meta-analysis replicates small-to-moderate benefits while flagging high bias and few active-control comparisons, reinforcing a Moderate ranking.

Description: Perform small concrete helpful acts and notice their effects on others and yourself.
Paper: Lim et al., 2024, Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology
Summary: Three RCTs (N=4,284): doing at least one act of kindness per week for 4 weeks produced small but significant reductions in loneliness and social isolation in the US and UK.
Claude Reasoning: Large multi-country RCT of the exact 'small acts of kindness' instruction shows real but small and country-inconsistent effects, squarely Moderate.

Description: Perform small concrete helpful acts and notice their effects on others and yourself.
Paper: Wieners et al., 2021, International Journal of Wellbeing
Summary: RCT, 222 students: a 4-week acts-of-kindness intervention raised mental wellbeing most when directed at strong social ties; positive-emotion/self-esteem mediation was not supported.
Claude Reasoning: On-target RCT showing kindness boosts wellbeing but effects depend on target and lack clear mechanisms, consistent with Moderate evidence.

Description: Perform small concrete helpful acts and notice their effects on others and yourself.
Paper: Haydon et al., 2023, The Journal of Positive Psychology
Summary: Pilot RCT, 137 breast cancer survivors: acts of kindness for others increased perceived social support, but no differences emerged on wellbeing or depressive symptoms.
Claude Reasoning: A pilot RCT with null primary outcomes included for honesty; it shows targeted benefits but mixed results, illustrating why kindness sits at Moderate.

Description: Privately write for 15–20 minutes about a stressful event and what it means.
Paper: Guo, 2022, British Journal of Clinical Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 31 RCTs (N=4,012): expressive writing had a small but significant effect on depression, anxiety and stress (g=-0.12), emerging at follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis of the exact Pennebaker paradigm shows a real but small, delayed effect, matching a Moderate ranking.

Description: Privately write for 15–20 minutes about a stressful event and what it means.
Paper: Pavlacic et al., 2019, Review of General Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis: expressive writing showed small effects on posttraumatic stress and negligible-to-small effects on growth and quality of life, but medium-large effects in diagnosed PTSD samples.
Claude Reasoning: Shows the writing-about-a-stressful-event construct helps modestly overall but more in clinical samples, supporting Moderate with honest scope limits.

Description: Privately write for 15–20 minutes about a stressful event and what it means.
Paper: Mogk et al., 2006, GMS Psycho-Social Medicine
Summary: Meta-analysis of 30 RCTs: found no significant effects of expressive writing on somatic or psychological health, contrary to earlier reported benefits.
Claude Reasoning: A null RCT-only meta-analysis included for balance; the conflicting findings across reviews are precisely why expressive writing is ranked Moderate, not strong.

Description: Write vividly about a realistic future in which important goals have gone well.
Paper: Carrillo et al., 2019, PLoS ONE
Summary: Meta-analysis, 29 studies (N=2,909): the Best Possible Self exercise improved wellbeing (d=.33), optimism (d=.33) and positive affect (d=.51) versus controls.
Claude Reasoning: Meta-analysis of the exact BPS writing exercise shows consistent small-to-moderate effects, directly justifying a Moderate ranking.

Description: Write vividly about a realistic future in which important goals have gone well.
Paper: Heekerens & Eid, 2020, The Journal of Positive Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 34 RCTs: BPS produced small effects on positive affect (g=0.28) and optimism (g=0.21), strongest immediately after, with no substantial follow-up effects.
Claude Reasoning: RCT-only meta-analysis confirms BPS works as a brief mood/expectation induction but without lasting effects, fitting a Moderate ranking.

Description: Write vividly about a realistic future in which important goals have gone well.
Paper: Malouff & Schutte, 2017, The Journal of Positive Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 29 RCTs (N=3,319): optimism interventions increased optimism (g=.41), with significantly larger effects for studies using the Best Possible Self exercise.
Claude Reasoning: Broader optimism-training meta-analysis singling out BPS as the most effective variant supports the construct, while moderator caveats keep it Moderate.

Description: Identify a character strength and use it in a new way each day.
Paper: Gander et al., 2024, Collabra: Psychology
Summary: RCT, N=1,163: 'using signature strengths in a new way' for one week increased wellbeing, social wellbeing, and character-strength states and traits versus a placebo control.
Claude Reasoning: Large RCT testing the exact 'use a strength in a new way' instruction against an active placebo shows real benefits, the strongest direct evidence for a Moderate ranking.

Description: Identify a character strength and use it in a new way each day.
Paper: Proyer et al., 2015, Frontiers in Psychology
Summary: Placebo-controlled RCT, 375 adults: using signature strengths in a new way increased happiness up to 3 months and reduced depression short-term versus placebo control.
Claude Reasoning: Classic placebo-controlled RCT of the daily 'strengths in a new way' exercise shows benefits that fade over time, consistent with Moderate evidence.

Description: Identify a character strength and use it in a new way each day.
Paper: Duan et al., 2018, Journal of Happiness Studies
Summary: RCT, 100 first-year students: a character-strengths intervention raised thriving and cut negative symptoms short-term (effects faded by 1 year); strengths use partially mediated outcomes.
Claude Reasoning: On-target RCT supporting strengths use as the active mechanism but with effects that did not persist to 1 year, reinforcing a Moderate ranking.

Description: Write a brief uncensored account of thoughts, decisions, and lessons each day.
Paper: Guo, 2022, British Journal of Clinical Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of 31 RCTs (N=4012). Expressive writing showed a small but significant reduction in depression, anxiety, and stress (Hedges' g=-0.12).
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of RCTs with long-term follow-up shows a real but small, delayed benefit of brief daily-style writing, matching a Moderate ranking.

Description: Write a brief uncensored account of thoughts, decisions, and lessons each day.
Paper: Reinhold et al., 2018, Clinical Psychology: Science and Practice
Summary: Meta-analysis of 39 RCTs (64 comparisons). Brief self-directed expressive writing did not yield significant long-term effects on depressive symptoms.
Claude Reasoning: This null-leaning meta-analysis honestly bounds the effect, showing why the support is only Moderate and mixed rather than strong.

Description: Write a brief uncensored account of thoughts, decisions, and lessons each day.
Paper: Pavlacic et al., 2019, Review of General Psychology
Summary: Meta-analysis of expressive writing found small effects on posttraumatic stress and negligible-to-small effects on growth and quality of life.
Claude Reasoning: Synthesis across outcomes shows generally small effects (larger only in diagnosed PTSD), consistent with a Moderate, on-target rating for everyday journaling.

Description: Do at least one tiny action toward the goal every day, even on bad days.
Paper: Bizzozero-Peroni et al., 2024, JAMA Network Open
Summary: Meta-analysis of 33 observational studies (N=96,173). Each +1000 daily steps was associated with lower depression risk (RR 0.91); >=5000 vs <5000 steps fewer symptoms.
Claude Reasoning: Large synthesis shows even small daily increments of activity track with better mood; observational design (no RCTs) fits a Moderate ranking for the 'tiny daily action' principle.

Description: Do at least one tiny action toward the goal every day, even on bad days.
Paper: Santopetro et al., 2023, JMIR Mental Health
Summary: RCT (N=126) of a 1-month daily SMS behavioral-activation prompt; enjoyable-activity condition cut depressive symptoms vs passive control.
Claude Reasoning: An RCT showing that prompting small daily activities reduces symptoms supports the mechanism, though single-trial scope keeps it Moderate.

Description: Do at least one tiny action toward the goal every day, even on bad days.
Paper: Benson et al., 2025, Journal of Technology in Behavioral Science
Summary: Pre-registered single-arm study (N=99) of a brief behavioral-activation micro-intervention; mood and depression scores improved at 3-day follow-up.
Claude Reasoning: A tiny-action micro-intervention improved mood, but the uncontrolled design and small effects keep the construct at Moderate.

Description: Expose yourself briefly to cold water, starting mild and short, and observe stress or mood effects.
Paper: Cain et al., 2025, PLOS ONE
Summary: Systematic review/meta-analysis of 11 RCTs (N=3177). Cold-water immersion reduced stress 12h post-exposure (SMD -1.00) but not immediately; no mood effect.
Claude Reasoning: Meta-analysis of RCTs shows time-dependent stress benefits but few trials, small samples, and null mood findings, squarely supporting a Moderate ranking.

Description: Expose yourself briefly to cold water, starting mild and short, and observe stress or mood effects.
Paper: Briganti et al., 2023, Acta Physiologica
Summary: PRISMA review of 24 studies (N=445) of cold-water exposure in non-athletes; psychological and mood effects were inconclusive with mixed stress-hormone results.
Claude Reasoning: A systematic review reporting conflicting and inconclusive psychological findings honestly bounds the evidence as mixed, consistent with Moderate.

Description: Follow a fixed pattern such as box breathing, 4-7-8 breathing, or cyclic hyperventilation with recovery.
Paper: Fincham et al., 2023, Scientific Reports
Summary: Meta-analysis of 12 RCTs (N=785) found breathwork lowered subjective stress vs controls (g=-0.35); similar small effects for anxiety and depression.
Claude Reasoning: A meta-analysis of RCTs on structured breathing shows a real small-to-medium benefit, with most trials at moderate risk of bias, matching a Moderate ranking.

Description: Follow a fixed pattern such as box breathing, 4-7-8 breathing, or cyclic hyperventilation with recovery.
Paper: Yilmaz Balban et al., 2023, Cell Reports Medicine
Summary: RCT comparing daily 5-min box breathing, cyclic sighing, and cyclic hyperventilation vs meditation; cyclic sighing best improved mood and lowered respiratory rate.
Claude Reasoning: This RCT directly tests the exact named protocols (box breathing, cyclic hyperventilation), showing modest mood gains, on-target support for a Moderate ranking.

Description: Follow a fixed pattern such as box breathing, 4-7-8 breathing, or cyclic hyperventilation with recovery.
Paper: Fincham et al., 2023, Scientific Reports
Summary: Largest breathwork RCT (N=400): coherent breathing at ~5.5 breaths/min showed no benefit over a matched breathing placebo on stress or mood.
Claude Reasoning: A well-powered placebo-controlled RCT finding no advantage over placebo tempers the evidence, justifying a Moderate rather than strong ranking.

Description: Notice craving sensations as waves, breathe, and wait without acting until they crest and fall.
Paper: Harris et al., 2017, Mindfulness
Summary: RCT (N=67 adolescents) adding 4 weeks of urge surfing as alcohol aftercare; significant reductions in drinking frequency and quantity vs waitlist.
Claude Reasoning: This RCT tests urge surfing by name, showing real but borderline-significant effects in a small sample, directly supporting a Moderate ranking.

Description: Notice craving sensations as waves, breathe, and wait without acting until they crest and fall.
Paper: Demina et al., 2023, BMC Neuroscience
Summary: Meta-analysis of 17 RCTs (N=1228) found mindfulness interventions reduced craving (SMD -0.70), but high heterogeneity (I2=92%) and low GRADE quality.
Claude Reasoning: Urge surfing is a core mindfulness craving skill; this broader meta-analysis shows a promising effect undercut by heterogeneity and bias, fitting Moderate.

Description: Notice craving sensations as waves, breathe, and wait without acting until they crest and fall.
Paper: Witkiewitz et al., 2012, Addictive Behaviors
Summary: Secondary analysis of an MBRP RCT; acceptance, awareness, and nonjudgment mediated lower post-treatment craving vs treatment-as-usual.
Claude Reasoning: Shows the acceptance-based mechanism behind urge surfing reduces craving, but as a mediation/secondary analysis it offers supportive, not definitive, Moderate evidence.

Description: Treat each small behavior as evidence for the identity you want to strengthen.
Paper: Rhodes et al., 2024, Health Psychology Review
Summary: Meta-analysis of 40 effect sizes (N=4939). Interventions changed physical-activity identity (g=0.18) and related behavior change (g=0.61), with possible publication bias.
Claude Reasoning: This meta-analysis shows identity is malleable and linked to behavior change, but small identity effects and bias signals support a Moderate ranking.

Description: Treat each small behavior as evidence for the identity you want to strengthen.
Paper: Zhu et al., 2025, Applied Psychology: Health and Well-Being
Summary: Three-level meta-analysis of 19 studies (N=13,340) found a large positive correlation between habit and identity in health behaviors (r=0.55).
Claude Reasoning: Confirms a strong habit-identity link supporting the technique's logic, but correlational (mostly cross-sectional) design keeps causal support Moderate.

Description: Treat each small behavior as evidence for the identity you want to strengthen.
Paper: de Hoog et al., 2024, Social Science & Medicine
Summary: Meta-analysis of 115 articles (248 effect sizes, N=112,112) found a small but positive association between social identity and health-related behavior.
Claude Reasoning: Broadens the identity-behavior link beyond personal to social identity with a small positive effect, supporting the principle at a Moderate level.

Description: Pick one habit likely to improve other behaviors or make other habits easier and work on developing that habit first.
Paper: Oaten & Cheng, 2006, British Journal of Health Psychology
Summary: Controlled study: a 2-month exercise program improved self-regulation and spilled over to less smoking, alcohol, caffeine and better diet, spending, and study habits.
Claude Reasoning: Directly demonstrates a single 'keystone' habit improving many unrelated behaviors, but the small, non-RCT design keeps it Moderate.

Description: Pick one habit likely to improve other behaviors or make other habits easier and work on developing that habit first.
Paper: Palmeira et al., 2025, Annals of Behavioral Medicine
Summary: Longitudinal study (N=678) over 12 months found a reciprocal motivational spillover between exercise and eating, though effects on actual steps/intake were small.
Claude Reasoning: Supports cross-domain spillover central to keystone habits, but mainly at the motivational level with weak behavioral transfer, fitting Moderate.

Description: Pick one habit likely to improve other behaviors or make other habits easier and work on developing that habit first.
Paper: Oh & Taylor, 2014, Health Psychology
Summary: Cross-over RCT (N=23 abstinent smokers): a single 15-min bout of exercise acutely reduced cravings and attentional bias for both cigarettes and snacks.
Claude Reasoning: Shows one habit (exercise) acutely dampening multiple cravings, an on-target spillover mechanism, though tiny-sample acute design supports only Moderate.

Description: Mark each day you complete the habit and try to preserve the streak.
Paper: Harkin et al., 2016, Psychological Bulletin
Summary: Meta-analysis of 138 studies (N=19,951). Progress monitoring promoted goal attainment (d+=0.40); effects were larger when records were physically recorded.
Claude Reasoning: Marking each day is physical progress monitoring; this large meta-analysis shows it reliably aids goal attainment with a small-to-moderate effect, fitting Moderate.

Description: Mark each day you complete the habit and try to preserve the streak.
Paper: Weathers et al., 2023, Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science
Summary: Survey, experimental, and field studies on activity streaks; commitment rose when streaks were framed as daily (vs cumulative) tasks and tied to identity.
Claude Reasoning: Directly studies the streak mechanism behind 'don't break the chain,' supporting daily framing, but mixed marketing-context designs keep it Moderate.

Description: Mark each day you complete the habit and try to preserve the streak.
Paper: Compernolle et al., 2019, Int. Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
Summary: Meta-analysis of 19 RCTs (N=2800): self-monitoring interventions reduced sedentary time (g=0.32), strongest with objective recording tools.
Claude Reasoning: Reinforces that habitual self-recording drives behavior change with small effects, supporting the chain-tracking technique at a Moderate level.

Description: Do the most aversive high-value task first in the workday.
Paper: Steel, 2007, Psychological Bulletin
Summary: Meta-analysis of 691 correlations identifying task aversiveness and task delay as strong, consistent predictors of procrastination.
Claude Reasoning: Establishes that task aversiveness drives delay, the rationale for tackling the most aversive task first; correlational meta-analysis supports the principle at Moderate.

Description: Do the most aversive high-value task first in the workday.
Paper: Tong et al., 2024, Acta Psychologica Sinica
Summary: 7-day longitudinal experiment (N=68); reducing task aversiveness via reappraisal raised task willingness and cut procrastination, mediated by lower aversiveness.
Claude Reasoning: Experimentally links lowering task aversiveness to acting sooner, supporting the 'eat the frog' rationale, but small and indirect, fitting Moderate.

Description: Do the most aversive high-value task first in the workday.
Paper: Blunt & Pychyl, 2000, Personality and Individual Differences
Summary: Study of 161 students found task aversiveness (boredom, frustration, resentment) positively related to procrastination across project stages.
